items.title: Don't hand-roll one in Python
items.avoid: Writing your own Node/LinkedList class for general use.
items.why: CPython's C-level implementations are dramatically faster than pure-Python nodes, and well-tested.
items.example:
```python
from collections import deque
recent = deque(maxlen=1000)   # ready-made sliding window
```

items.title: Pick the structure by the operation that dominates
items.avoid: Defaulting to one structure for every workload.
items.prefer: Random access → list; insert-anywhere → linked list; both ends → deque; key-ordered → OrderedDict.
items.why: Each structure has one or two operations it does in O(1); the rest can be O(n) or worse.
items.example:
```python
# Hot path: index by position -> list
# Hot path: pop from front -> deque
# Hot path: move-to-end + evict oldest -> OrderedDict
```

items.title: Use Floyd's algorithm to detect cycles
items.avoid: Walking a chain with no cycle check on user-supplied data.
items.prefer: Tortoise-and-hare: two pointers, one fast, one slow.
items.why: Detects cycles in O(n) time, O(1) extra space, no need to remember visited nodes.
items.example:
```python
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast: return True
    return False
```

items.title: Use sentinels to simplify edge cases
items.avoid: Tangled `if head is None: ... elif cur is None: ...` branches.
items.prefer: A dummy head/tail node so inserts and removals never special-case the boundaries.
items.why: Fewer null checks; harder to miss an edge case.
items.example:
```python
dummy = Node(None, head)
# walk from `dummy`; the real head is dummy.next
```
